forecasts.reasoning: South Sudan signed the AfCFTA and the treaty has been pending before its National Assembly since 2021. However, as of January 2025, South Sudan remained one of the seven African nations yet to ratify the agreement. Recent political developments show that South Sudan's parliament recently approved an extension of the transitional government by 24 months, indicating severe political gridlock, overriding domestic priorities, and dysfunction. There is no trace of recent news from late 2024 or early 2025 to suggest that ratifying the AfCFTA is currently on the active legislative agenda. Given the massive delays so far, the extremely short time horizon (less than two months between the May 2025 knowledge cutoff and the July 2025 resolution date), and the ongoing political crisis within the country, it is highly unlikely that South Sudan will finalize ratification before July 1, 2025.

As of the May 2, 2025 knowledge cutoff, there is just under two months until the July 1, 2025 resolution date. Currently, no date for an independence referendum has been officially scheduled. While Republika Srpska under Milorad Dodik has escalated separatist moves significantly in early 2025—including passing legislation to bar national police and judiciary—Dodik has also been convicted by a state court and banned from politics for six years, prompting articles discussing his "fall". Historically, Dodik's strategy consists of brinksmanship and loud threats of referendums without officially scheduling them, as setting a formal date for *explicit independence* crosses an extreme red line guaranteed to trigger immense international blowback and potential intervention. With Dodik fighting for his political survival and less than 60 days remaining in the question's timeline, the likelihood that RS officially sets a date for an independence referendum before July 2025 is extremely low.

Given the knowledge cutoff of May 2, 2025, the probability that the Scottish Government will officially announce a referendum date before July 1, 2025 is extremely low. Following the SNP's severe losses in the July 2024 UK General Election and the 2022 Supreme Court ruling that blocked unilateral referendums, First Minister John Swinney has overtly shifted the party's strategy from demanding immediate votes (such as Sturgeon's legislative attempts or the "de facto" election strategy) toward a long-term goal of building "compelling" popular support. While a mid-April 2025 poll gave the 'Yes' side an 11-point lead, there is virtually no runway — either legally or politically — for Swinney to pivot instantly and officially schedule a referendum within the following two months. Polling from March 2025 shows 72% of Scots oppose a referendum within the next year. It remains possible they announce a stunt date to build momentum for the 2026 Holyrood elections, but given Swinney's cautious style and the recent shift in SNP strategy, this is highly unlikely.

Greenland held a parliamentary election on March 11, 2025. Moving against the incumbent government that had hinted at an independence referendum, the center-right opposition party Demokraatit won the election. Led by Jens-Frederik Nielsen, Demokraatit explicitly advocates for a gradual approach to independence, emphasizing the need for economic self-sufficiency before considering sovereignty. The new governing coalition, finalized on March 28, 2025, holds 23 of the 31 seats but explicitly excluded the only fiercely pro-independence, pro-immediate-referendum party (Naleraq). While all parties in Greenland favor independence eventually, Nielsen and his allies maintain that any process will be slow and measured. The political focus has been on economic development and presenting unified pushback against US annexation threats, rather than setting a speedy referendum timeline. With the resolution date set for July 1, 2025 — leaving less than two months — there is virtually no domestic political urgency or intent from the newly installed gradualist government to announce a specific referendum date. Therefore, the probability is extremely low.

A formal and legal ban on a political party in Germany can legally only be issued by the Federal Constitutional Court (Bundesverfassungsgericht). In the history of the Federal Republic, only two parties have been banned (SRP in 1952 and KPD in 1956). Modern attempts to ban the NPD took several years each (2001-2003 and 2013-2017).

For the AfD, a cross-party motion to apply for a ban was debated in the Bundestag on January 30, 2025. However, there was no vote, because there was no majority. Furthermore, the German federal elections were scheduled for February 2025, and there is an understanding that passing such a motion in the new Bundestag will be even harder due to the AfD's projected strength. 

Even if the Bundesrat, Bundestag, or Federal Government were to officially file an application to ban the AfD tomorrow, the Constitutional Court would require extensive evidentiary hearings, expert testimonies, and deliberations. It is completely unprecedented and structurally impossible for the Court to process and rule on banning a major political party in fewer than 6 months. Therefore, an official announcement of a ban on the AfD by July 1, 2025, has a probability practically indistinguishable from zero.

The question asks if the Global Protest Tracker will record a US protest of 10,000+ participants between the question launch (mid-April 2025) and July 1, 2025. The Tracker focuses on major anti-government protests. While the US sees occasional massive protests, especially around inaugurations (January 2025 had the People's March), generating a new 10,000+ person anti-government protest in the remaining 2.5 months of this window (mid-April to end of June) without a scheduled major event is unlikely. The Tracker also doesn't update continuously (last spotted update was Nov 2024), and may have specific inclusion/exclusion criteria that omit typical annual marches (like Pride) unless they are strictly anti-government. Given the narrow time window and lack of currently identified major planned protests, the probability is relatively low.

For this question to resolve as YES, several independent conditions must be met:
1. A new qualifying mass protest (10,000+) must start in India between mid-April and July 1, 2025 (the currently ongoing Waqf protests do not count). 
2. The protest must be classified as an anti-government protest to fit the Global Protest Tracker's criteria.
3. Carnegie Endowment must release an update to the Tracker before the July 1 resolution date.
4. That update must cover the very recent period up to May/June without the usual extensive coding lag that plagues academic indices. 

Search queries show the Carnegie Global Protest Tracker is manually updated on a periodic basis—such as the November 14, 2024 update. Trackers of this nature typically update semi-annually or annually, and with a considerable data lag (usually several months) to allow researchers to verify crowd sizes, triggers, and outcomes. Even if an update is scheduled before July 1, 2025, it is overwhelmingly likely that its data cutoff will precede any new protests emerging in late spring/early summer. Thus, the conjunctive probability of a new massive protest occurring, being tracked, coded, and published on the platform within this short window is virtually zero. I am assigning a 3% probability.

Extensive searches reveal that as of early May 2025, Uber does not operate in the Turks and Caicos Islands (TCI), and there are no announcements of an impending launch. Moreover, the local regulatory context is highly prohibitive: taxi driving is legally reserved for TCI nationals, and in April 2025, the local government launched a crackdown explicitly penalizing businesses for supporting 'illegal taxis' or unlicensed transport operators. The likelihood of legislation changing, an agreement being struck with the strong local taxi lobby, and Uber successfully launching within the less than two months before July 1, 2025, is practically zero. Therefore, the probability of Uber being available in the Turks and Caicos Islands on the resolution date is estimated at the 2% minimum.

Niger was recently upgraded from Level 4 to Level 3 in March 2025. Given that it was just downgraded and there are only about 2 months left (until July 1, 2025) for it to be moved back to Level 4, the probability is quite low unless a major negative event occurs such as another coup or significant terror attack targeting US citizens. State Department advisories usually stay stable for at least 6 months after a major review, and Niger's was just definitively moved to Level 3 on March 21, 2025. Thus, the chance it goes back to Level 4 before July 2025 is low.

Multiple U.S. Embassy in Tanzania pages (including security alerts from March 2025) and State Department mirror sources confirm that on January 21, 2025, the U.S. State Department increased Tanzania's travel advisory level from Level 2 to Level 3 ("Reconsider Travel") due to the addition of an "unrest" risk indicator. Because this event has already occurred well before the July 1, 2025 deadline, the resolution criteria have been decisively met, resulting in a practically certain "Yes" resolution.

The primary driver of unrest in Mozambique was the disputed late-2024 election. Even at the height of the protests and violence (over 300 reported dead between October 2024 and January 2025), the US State Department only escalated its travel advisory to Level 3 ("Reconsider Travel"). The new president was inaugurated in mid-January 2025, and subsequent reporting from February and March 2025 indicates a general calming of the situation, leading other nations to lift travel warnings and the US Embassy to reportedly downgrade back to a Level 2 advisory by April. Since a Level 4 ("Do Not Travel") advisory requires a catastrophic, nation-wide collapse in security (e.g., active civil war, severe pandemic)—which did not occur even during the peak of recent unrest—the probability of reaching Level 4 before July 2025 is negligible. Only an entirely unexpected new national catastrophe could trigger this within the brief 60-day remaining window.

As of April 25, 2025, the US State Department travel advisory for Ethiopia was reissued at 'Level 3: Reconsider Travel'. There are localized 'Do Not Travel' warnings for specific regions due to ongoing conflicts (such as in Amhara), but the nationwide advisory remains at Level 3. With the knowledge cutoff at May 2, 2025, there are less than 60 days before the July 1, 2025 resolution date. A spontaneous escalation to a nationwide 'Level 4: Do Not Travel' advisory within a two-month period would require a massive and sudden deterioration of security across the entire country (e.g., a threat to the capital causing an embassy evacuation). Given the short time frame and the recent reaffirmation of the Level 3 status, the likelihood of an escalation to Level 4 before July 1, 2025, is exceptionally low.
